answer:
In Cynewulf's poems (*Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*), the Old English term *lof*—denoting praise, glory, or fame—predominantly carries a Christianized valence, reframed as steadfast praise (*lof*) of God or heavenly glory amid suffering, rather than the pagan-heroic ideal of worldly renown earned through martial prowess.[^1] This aligns with the saints' humble endurance of torment, subordinating earthly *lof* (fame/glory) to divine will and implicitly critiquing vainglory (*idelse gielp*, or empty boast) by emphasizing inner spiritual fortitude over external acclaim. No explicit condemnation of pagan *lof* as vainglory appears (unlike *Vainglory* in the *Old English Elegies*), but the motif recurs in contexts of trial, where *lof* binds the heart (*fyrhthlocan*) unyieldingly to Christ despite physical ruin, modeling humility.[^2]

### Praise (*lof*) Amid Martyrdom
- In *Andreas*, Matthew—facing cannibalistic torment—holds "Cristes lof... faeste bewunden" (*Christ's praise... fast bound*) in his breast, praising God even as fiends prepare to devour him.[^3] Andreas echoes this, praising (*wyrthode... lof*) the heavenly King post-torture, his body "sare geswungen" (sore-struck) yet spirit unbroken.[^4] Heroic *lof* (fame from battle) yields to humble *lof* of the "wuldres aldor" (Glory-Lord), as saints reject vainglory for eternal reward.
- *Juliana*'s saint binds "Cristes lof... faeste biwunden" (*Christ's praise... fast bound*) in her heart during flaying and boiling, her "milde modsefan" (gentle mind) unmoved by the devil's temptations to idolatry.[^5] Her refusal of the pagan judge's *lof* (honor via marriage/wealth) embodies humility: earthly glory is "unbithyrfe" (worthless) next to God's.
- *Elene* links *lof* to prophetic praise of Christ, with Judas reciting David and Isaiah on the Incarnation amid Jewish denial.[^6] Post-conversion, communal *lof* rises (*lof hafen*) at the True Cross's discovery, humility (*eathmedum*) prevailing over prideful concealment.[^7]

### Humility and Eternal *Lof*
Cynewulf integrates *lof* with *eathmodnes* (humility), as saints pray amid agony (e.g., Andreas begs release from "edwitspræce" [reproach] yet praises unceasingly).[^8] This counters pagan *lof*-seeking (fame via deeds), as in heroic contexts, by prioritizing heavenly *lof*: the poet's runic signatures (e.g., *Andreas* end) plead for prayers to earn "ece lif" (eternal life), humbly trading temporal glory for divine.[^9] No pagan "warrior fame" is valorized; *lof* is God's due, vainglory absent as saints' "modsefa... geblissod" (mind gladdened) in submission.[^10]

The corpus lacks direct pagan-Christian *lof*-clash debates, but Cynewulf poetically resolves it: humility transforms *lof* from fleeting fame to enduring praise.[^11]

[^1]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [opening](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/transliteration/#p-992aeb/); *Juliana*, ch. 1, [p-01394b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/transliteration/#p-01394b/); *Elene*, ch. 1, [p-624562](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/transliteration/#p-624562/).

[^2]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [humility passage](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1/).

[^3]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-992aeb (translit.)](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/transliteration/#p-992aeb/); trans.: "Him wæs Cristes lof on fyrhthlocan faeste bewunden."

[^4]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, wiki key passage [p-74e29a](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-74e29a/).

[^5]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [p-01394b (translit.)](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/transliteration/#p-01394b/); wiki: "raise his praise."

[^6]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [p-624562](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/transliteration/#p-624562/).

[^7]: *Elene*, ch. 1, closing: "lof hafen thrymme micle."

[^8]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-992aeb](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/transliteration/#p-992aeb/).

[^9]: *Andreas* wiki: trigrams like "wuldres aldor" (glory-lord); signatures seek prayer-*lof*.

[^10]: *Andreas* wiki full: unigrams "glory," collocs "fray + struggle."

[^11]: Cf. *Old English Elegies* "Vainglory" for explicit anti-vainglory, outside Cynewulf.

args.query: lof humility OR vainglory OR pride cynewulf
